items.passage: Seed dispersal is the natural process by which plants spread their offspring away from the parent to new ground. Many flowering plants wrap their seeds inside sweet fruit that hungry animals eat and carry off. Others grow tiny hooks that cling to fur or feathers as creatures brush past them. Lightweight seeds often drift on the wind, floating great distances before they finally settle. This scattering lets young plants avoid crowding and reach fresh soil where sunlight is plentiful.

Sustainable agriculture aims to grow food in ways that protect the land for future generations of farmers. Instead of relying only on chemicals, many growers rotate their crops to keep the soil rich. Cover plants are often planted between seasons to stop valuable topsoil from washing away. Farmers also welcome insects and birds that naturally control the pests damaging their fields. These careful methods reduce pollution and help ensure that harvests remain steady for many years ahead.

Creativity is the mental ability to combine familiar ideas in fresh ways that produce something genuinely new. Psychologists once believed this rare gift belonged only to a handful of talented artists. Research now shows that nearly everyone can strengthen their imagination through practice and open curiosity. A relaxed, playful mood often allows the brain to link thoughts it would normally keep separate. By exploring many possible answers instead of one, people frequently stumble upon truly original solutions.

The Industrial Revolution was a dramatic period when machines began replacing the slow labor of human hands. Powerful steam engines drove new factories that produced cloth and iron far faster than before. Crowds of workers moved from quiet villages into growing cities to find steady wages. Railways soon carried goods and passengers across whole countries in a single remarkable day. Although life in the early factories was harsh, this age transformed how modern societies live and work.

Deep inside the Earth's crust lie minerals, the solid building blocks from which every rock is made. Each type has a fixed chemical recipe and a neat internal pattern of repeating atoms. Geologists tell them apart by testing features like hardness, color, and the shine of a fresh surface. Many minerals crystallize slowly as molten rock cools far beneath the ground over long ages. Others appear when mineral-rich water dries up, leaving delicate crystals lodged within narrow cracks.

Streaming silently through space, cosmic rays are among the fastest particles known anywhere in the universe. They are hurled outward by exploding stars and other violent events scattered across distant galaxies. When such a particle slams into the upper air, it shatters into a shower of lighter fragments. Sensitive instruments carried by satellites and balloons record these faint arrivals for careful study. By measuring cosmic rays, researchers uncover clues about forces far stronger than any found on Earth.
